1: bārekî nafshî ʾet-YHWH YHWH ʾĕlōhay gādaltā mĕʾōd — bless the LORD, O my soul! O LORD my God, how great you are. The self-address of Ps 103:1 opens the creation hymn. Gādaltā mĕʾōd (you are very great — the exclamatory recognition of divine greatness in the face of creation's scope). hôd wĕhādār lābāshtā — robed in honor and majesty. Hôd (splendor, majesty — the radiant glory of the divine presence) + hādār (honor, ornamental beauty). The divine Creator clothed in the qualities of creation's own beauty.
2: ʿōṭeh-ʾôr kaśśalmāh — wrapped in light as in a robe. Light is not created (as in Gen 1:3 where it is the first created thing) but worn as a garment — the divine self-clothing in light precedes creation. The light is the outward expression of the divine glory, the garment that makes YHWH visible. nôṭeh shāmayim kayyĕrîʿāh — spreading out the heavens like a tent curtain. Yĕrîʿāh (tent curtain — the fabric panels of a tent). The heavens stretched like tent fabric by the divine hand — the image of a powerful deity pitching a cosmic tent (cf. Isa 40:22: hantôṭeh kaddôq shāmayim).
3: hameqāreh bammayim ʿăliyyôtāyw — he sets the beams of his upper chambers on the waters. Qārāh (to set beams — the laying of the ceiling beams of a building) on the waters above the firmament (Gen 1:7's waters above). The divine palace above the heavenly waters — the upper rooms (ʿăliyyôt) of the cosmic palace. haśśām ʿābîm rĕkhûbô hamehallek ʿal-kanpê-rûaḥ — he mounts the clouds as his chariot; he travels on the wings of the wind. ʿĀb (thick cloud) as divine chariot — the storm-cloud as the vehicle of the divine warrior (cf. Ps 68:4, 33; Isa 19:1). Kanpê-rûaḥ (wings of the wind) — the wind as the speed of divine movement.
4: ʿōśeh malʾākhāyw rûḥôt mĕshārĕtāyw ʾēsh lōhēṭ — he makes the winds his messengers and flashing fire his servants. The deliberate ambiguity: malʾākhāyw rûḥôt can be read as either (a) he makes his angels into winds (the angels are the winds) or (b) he makes the winds his messengers/angels. Both readings are grammatically valid. Heb 1:7 reads this in sense (a) — angels as winds — applying it to the Son's superiority over angels. The psalm's point in either case: the natural forces (wind, fire) are divine servants, not independent powers or deities.
5: yāsad-ʾereṣ ʿal-mĕkhônêhā — he fixed the earth on its foundations. Yāsad (to found, lay the foundation — the architect's act of establishing the base). Mĕkhôn (foundation, established base — from kûn: to be fixed). bal-timmôṭ ʿôlām wāʿed — so that it will never be shaken. Mûṭ (to shake, totter — the destabilization of what is fixed). The double temporal scope: ʿôlām wāʿed (forever and ever). The earth's stability as a consequence of divine founding — it holds because of the divine action that established it.
6: tĕhôm kallib-bûsh kissîtô — you covered it with the deep like a cloak. Tĕhôm (the deep — the primordial ocean of Gen 1:2; the deep that covered the pre-creation earth). Libûsh (clothing, garment) — the deep draped over the earth like a cloak (echoing the divine light-clothing of v2: YHWH wears light; the earth is clothed in the deep). ʿal-hārîm yaʿamdû māyim — the waters towered above the mountains. The primordial state: waters covering even the mountain peaks — the pre-creation condition before the divine ordering of v7-9.
7: min-gĕʿārātĕkhā yānûsûn — at your rebuke they retreated. Gāʿar (to rebuke — the commanding voice that drives away; cf. Ps 106:9 where the sea is rebuked at the Exodus). The waters flee at the divine rebuke — the chaos-ordering by divine voice. min-qôl raʿamĕkhā yēḥāpēzûn — at the sound of your thunder they fled in haste. Raʿam (thunder — the divine voice in the storm) causing the waters to flee in terror (ḥāpaz: to be in hasty flight). The creation of order is described in the language of a theophany — the storm-God commanding the chaotic waters.
8: yaʿălû hārîm yērĕdû bĕqāʿôt ʾel-mĕqôm zeh yāsadtā lāhem — the mountains rose and the valleys sank to the places you had set for them. The topographic ordering: mountains rising, valleys descending — the creation of the varied landscape. Mĕqôm zeh yāsadtā lāhem (the place you founded for them) — each geological feature placed exactly where YHWH designed it. The creative act as precise architectural intentionality.
9: gĕbûl śamtā bal-yaʿăbōrûn — you drew a boundary they cannot cross. Gĕbûl (boundary, border — the territorial limit set by divine decree) + lōʾ yaʿăbōrûn (they shall not cross it — the permanent prohibition on the chaos-waters). bal-yĕkhassûn lĕkhassôt hāʾāreṣ — so the waters will never again engulf the earth. The covenant promise echoing the Noahic covenant (Gen 9:11-15): the waters will not again cover the earth. The creation-order is protected by divine decree — the chaos does not simply retreat but is permanently bounded.
10: hamekhallēaḥ maʿyānîm bannĕḥālîm — he sends springs into the valleys. The transition from creation's ordering (vv5-9) to creation's provision (vv10-18): water in the right place (springs in valleys, not covering mountains) becomes the source of life. Maʿyān (spring of water) sent into nĕḥālîm (valleys, wadis — the dry riverbeds that fill with spring water). bên hārîm yĕhallēkhûn — they run between the hills. The springs becoming streams flowing through the hill country — the water system threading through the created landscape.
11: yashqû kol-ḥaytô śādāy — giving water to every wild animal. Shāqāh (to water, give drink) — the springs providing water to all the wild animals. yishbĕrû pĕrāʾîm ṣĕmāʾām — the wild donkeys slake their thirst there. Pereh (wild donkey/onager — the untameable wild ass of the open steppe; Job 39:5-8 describes its freedom). The wild, uncatchable creature finds its thirst quenched by the Creator's springs — even the wildest animals are provided for.
12: ʿălêhem ʿôph hashshāmayim yishkôn — along their banks the birds of the sky make their homes. Shākan (to dwell, tabernacle — the verb of the divine dwelling among Israel; here the birds of the sky dwelling along the streams). mibbên ʿŏphāyim yittĕnû-qôl — and sing among the branches. The birdsong among the branches (ʿŏphāyim: branches, foliage) as part of the created order's praise — the birds' song as creation's voice.
13: mashqeh hārîm mēʿăliyyôtāyw — from his upper chambers he sends rain down the mountains. ʿĂliyyôt (upper chambers — the same divine palace-rooms of v3 from which the cloud-chariot departs). The rain comes from the divine palace above the clouds — the water cycle as divine provision from the heavenly dwelling. mippĕrî maʿăśêkhā tiśbaʿ hāʾāreṣ — the earth is filled by what he provides. Mippĕrî maʿăśêkhā (from the fruit of your works) — the earth satisfied by the fruit of the divine creative activity. Creation's abundance is always the ongoing result of divine action, not an independent system.
14: maṣmîaḥ ḥāṣîr labbĕhēmāh wĕʿēśeb laʿăbōdat hāʾādām — he makes grass grow for the cattle and plants for people to tend. Ṣāmaḥ (hiphil: to cause to sprout — the divine causation of vegetative growth). Ḥāṣîr labbĕhēmāh (grass for the cattle) + ʿēśeb laʿăbōdat hāʾādām (vegetation for the labor/service of humanity — the agricultural plants that human work cultivates). lĕhôṣîʾ leḥem min-hāʾāreṣ — so they can bring food out of the ground. The partnership: God causes growth, humans labor, bread comes from the ground. Divine and human action together in the provision of food.
15: wĕyayin yĕśammah lĕbab-ʾĕnôsh — wine that lifts the human heart. Śāmaḥ lēbāb (to gladden the heart) — wine as a created good, explicitly approved by the Creator as something that produces the gladness of the human heart. lĕhahălîl pānîm mishshāmen — oil that makes the face glow. Hālal pānîm (to make the face shine — the radiant, health-full face) from the oil. wĕleḥem lĕbab-ʾĕnôsh yisʿād — and bread that gives strength to the human heart. Sāʿad (to support, sustain — bread as the fundamental support of the body and the heart). The three gifts (wine, oil, bread) represent the full range of agricultural provision — festive, cosmetic, and nutritive.
16: yiśbĕʿû ʿăṣê YHWH ʾarzê lĕbānôn ʾăsher nāṭāʿ — the trees of the LORD drink deeply — the great cedars of Lebanon that he himself planted. ʿĂṣê YHWH (the trees of the LORD — trees belonging to YHWH by his planting) = the cedars of Lebanon (ʾarzê lĕbānôn). Nāṭaʿ (to plant — the divine planting of the great trees). The magnificent cedars are not self-seeded but divinely planted — God as the forester who planted the greatest trees on earth. They are yiśbĕʿûn (satisfied/well-watered) — provided for as YHWH provides for all living things.
17: ʾăsher shām ṣippārîm yĕqannēnû — there the birds build their nests. The great cedars as habitat — the magnificent trees become the homes of the birds. Qānan (to build a nest — the nesting act). ḥăsîdāh bĕrôshîm bêtāh — the stork finds her home in the cypress trees. Ḥăsîdāh (the stork — from ḥesed: the stork known for its loyalty/devotion to its young; the bird named for covenant-love). Bĕrôsh (cypress or juniper). Even the stork — whose name carries the root of covenant loyalty — is provided for in the divinely-planted trees.
18: hārîm haggĕbōhîm layye-ʿēlîm — the rugged mountains belong to the wild goats. Yaʿēl (mountain goat/ibex — the wild goat adapted to the steep rocky terrain). sĕlāʿîm maḥseh lashshĕphannîm — the rocky crags shelter the rock badgers. Shāphān (rock badger/hyrax — the small mammal that lives in rock crevices). The full range of habitats — tall cedars, mountains, rocky crags — each provided with its appropriate inhabitants. The ecosystems of creation as divine arrangement, not random distribution.
19: ʿāśāh yārēaḥ lĕmôʿădîm — he appointed the moon to mark the seasons. Môʿēd (appointed time, season, festival — the calendar system). The moon created specifically for the purpose of marking the calendar (lĕmôʿădîm: for the appointed seasons). shemesh yādaʿ mĕbôʾô — the sun knows exactly when to set. Yādaʿ mābôʾ (knows its setting — the sun as an obedient servant that knows its time). Both luminaries created for their functions, not worshiped as deities — the radical demythologization of the astral powers in Israelite cosmology (cf. Gen 1:14-18 where they are named merely greater light and lesser light).
20: tāshēt ḥōshekh wayĕhî lāylāh — you bring on darkness and it becomes night. The divine causing of night — YHWH as the one who sets (shît) the darkness into place. Night is not the absence of divine activity but a divinely appointed time. bô-tirmōś kol-ḥaytô-yāʿar — when all the forest creatures stir. Rāmaś (to creep, crawl, move about — especially of the smaller animals). The night as the time appointed for the forest animals' activity — the nocturnal ecosystem as part of the divine design.
21: hakkĕphîrîm shōʾăgîm lataref — the young lions roar as they hunt. Kĕphîr (young lion — the lion in its prime hunting strength). Shāʾag (to roar — the hunting roar of the lion). ûlĕbaqshēsh mēʾēl ʾākĕlām — looking to God for their food. Bāqash mēʾēl (seeking from God) — even the lion's hunting is described as seeking food from God. The predator's hunger and the act of hunting are within the providential framework of the Creator's provision. Not a theological problem but a theological observation: the whole creation, including its predatory activity, is encompassed in divine providence.
22: tizraḥ hashshemesh yēʾāsēphûn — when the sun comes up, they slip away. The transition at sunrise: the nocturnal hunters return to their lairs. ʾāsaph (niphal: to be gathered, to withdraw — the lions withdrawing as the sun rises). wĕʾel-mĕʿônōtām yirĕbāṣûn — and stretch out in their lairs. Rābaṣ (to crouch, lie down — the resting posture of an animal in its den). The daily rhythm of animal life governed by the sun's rising and setting — the clock set by the Creator in v19 regulates the whole ecosystem.
23: yēṣēʾ ʾādām lĕphōʿŏlô wĕlaʿăbōdātô ʿădê-ʿāreb — then people go out to their work and labor on until the evening. ʾĀdām (humanity — the human sphere of activity). Phōʿal (work, labor) + ʿăbōdāh (service, labor — the human labor to which people go out at sunrise). The human labor-day framed within the same creation order that governs the lions' night — humans and lions share the same divinely ordered world, each active in its appointed time.
24: māh-rabbû maʿăśêkhā YHWH kullām bĕḥokhĕmāh ʿāśîtā — LORD, what a multitude of works you have made! You crafted them all in wisdom. Māh-rabbû (how many! — the exclamatory response to creation's abundance). Bĕḥokhĕmāh ʿāśîtā (you made all of them in wisdom — ḥokhĕmāh as the divine design-principle behind creation; cf. Prov 8:22-31 where Wisdom is personified as present at creation). māleʾāh hāʾāreṣ qinyanekā — the earth is full of what belongs to you. Qinyan (acquisition, possession, creature — what God has acquired/created). The earth as YHWH's full possession.
25: zeh hayyām gādôl ûrĕḥab yādāyim — and here is the sea — vast and wide. Zeh (this — the demonstrative pointing as if to say: look at that). Gādôl ûrĕḥab yādāyim (great and wide of hands/dimensions — the spatial immensity of the sea). shām remes wĕʾên mispār ḥayyôt qĕṭannôt ʿim-gĕdōlôt — teeming with creatures beyond counting, great and small alike. Remes (swarming, creeping things — the creatures that swarm in the sea). ʾên mispār (no counting — the innumerable abundance). Great and small together: the ecosystem's full range of scale.
26: shām ʾoniyyôt yĕhallēkhûn lĕwiyātān zeh-yāṣartā lĕśaḥeq-bô — the ships sail there, and there is Leviathan, which you formed to frolic in the deep. Lĕwiyātān (Leviathan — the chaos dragon of Job 41, Ps 74:14, Isa 27:1; the sea-monster that represents primordial chaos in the ancient Near Eastern imagination). Here the psalm radically domesticates the chaos monster: zeh-yāṣartā lĕśaḥeq-bô (this one you formed to play in it). Leviathan is not an enemy to be defeated (as in Ps 74:14 or the Ugaritic Baal mythology) but a creature YHWH formed (yāṣar: the potter's forming) specifically to play (śāḥaq: to sport, play) in the sea. The most feared creature in ancient mythology becomes God's pet — the ultimate demythologization of chaos.
27: kullām ʾēlêkhā yĕśabbērûn lātēt ʾokhĕlām bĕʿittô — all of these look to you to give them their food when they need it. Śābar ʾēl (to look/wait expectantly toward — the posture of patient expectation directed toward God). All the creatures of vv10-26 — wild donkeys, birds, storks, ibex, lions, sea creatures, Leviathan itself — all looking to YHWH for their food. Bĕʿittô (in its time — the providential timing of the divine provision). Creation is not self-sustaining but continuously dependent on the Creator.
28: tittēn lāhem yilqôṭûn tiphttaḥ yādĕkhā yiśbĕʿûn ṭôb — when you give, they gather it in; when you open your hand, they are satisfied with good. Yalqaṭ (to gather — as grain is gathered from the field). Pātaḥ yād (to open the hand — the gesture of generous giving; cf. Ps 145:16: the divine open hand satisfying the desire of every living thing). Śābaʿ ṭôb (to be satisfied with good) — the same satisfaction sought in human prayer answered in the ongoing divine provision to all creatures.
29: tastîr pānêkhā yibbāhēlûn — if you hide your face, they are terrified. Sātar pānim (to hide the face — the divine withdrawal of attention and favor; cf. Ps 88:14; 102:2). When the divine face is hidden, the creatures are terrified (bāhal: to be dismayed, thrown into panic). tōsēph rûḥām yigwāʿûn wĕʾel-ʿăphārām yāshûbûn — if you take back their breath, they die and return to dust. Rûaḥ (breath, spirit) given or taken by God — the divine in-breathing and out-taking as the generator and terminator of life. ʿĂphār (dust — returning to Gen 3:19's dust). The Creator's breath sustains; its withdrawal ends.
30: tishlaḥ rûḥăkhā yibbārēʾûn ûtĕḥaddēsh pĕnê ʾădāmāh — when you send out your Spirit, they are created, and you renew the surface of the earth. Shālaḥ rûaḥ (to send out the Spirit/breath) + bārāʾ (to create — the Genesis 1 creation verb). The continuous creation: YHWH sending out his Spirit continually creates new life. Tĕḥaddēsh pĕnê ʾădāmāh (you renew the face of the earth) — the renewal of the created surface. The Spirit of Gen 1:2 (rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm mĕraḥephet ʿal-pĕnê hammāyim) as the ongoing creative force in creation, not merely a past event.
31: yĕhî khĕbôd YHWH lĕʿôlām yiśmaḥ YHWH bĕmaʿăśāyw — may the glory of the LORD last forever; may the LORD take delight in what he has made! Kĕbôd YHWH lĕʿôlām (the glory of YHWH forever) — the prayer that the divine glory, the weight and presence of God, endure eternally. Yiśmaḥ YHWH bĕmaʿăśāyw (may YHWH take delight/rejoice in his works) — echoing Gen 1:31 (wayyarʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-kol-ʾăsher ʿāśāh wĕhinnēh ṭôb mĕʾōd: God saw all he had made and it was very good). The petition that the divine delight in creation continue — a relational prayer asking that the Creator-creature relationship of pleasure persist.
32: hammaббîṭ lāʾāreṣ wattirʿad yiggaʿ bĕhārîm wĕyeʿĕshānû — he looks at the earth and it shakes; he touches the mountains and they pour out smoke. The theophanic power: divine looking causes earthquakes; divine touching causes volcanic eruption. The same power that ordered creation (vv5-9) can unmake its structures with a glance. The Creator's power over creation is not merely historical but perpetual and immediate.
33: ʾāshîrāh laYHWH bĕḥayyāy ʾăzammĕrāh lĕʾĕlōhay bĕʿôdî — I will sing to the LORD as long as I live; I will sing praise to my God for as long as I draw breath. Bĕḥayyāy (while I live — the full span of the Psalmist's life dedicated to praise). Bĕʿôdî (while I yet am — while I exist, while there is still breath). The personal vow: the lifetime of the individual Psalmist devoted to singing and praise. The cosmic creation hymn narrows at the end to the single human voice committed to praise for life.
34: yĕʿerab ʿālāyw śîḥî ʾānōkhî ʾeśmaḥ baYHWH — may my thoughts about him be sweet to him, as I find my joy in the LORD. ʿĀrēb (to be sweet, pleasing — the flavor-word for something delightful). Śîaḥ (meditation, reflection — the murmured meditation of Ps 77:3, 6; 104:34). The prayer that the Psalmist's meditation be pleasing (ʿārēb) to YHWH — the inner life offered as an acceptable offering. ʾĔśmaḥ baYHWH (I will rejoice in the LORD) — the personal joy grounded in the Creator who has just been celebrated across 33 verses.
35: yittammû ḥaṭṭāʾîm min-hāʾāreṣ ûrĕshāʿîm ʿôd ʾênam — let sinners vanish from the earth and let the wicked exist no longer! The jarring petition at the creation hymn's end — the one discordant note in the celebration of created order. Sinners (ḥaṭṭāʾîm) and the wicked (rĕshāʿîm) are the creatures who mar what has been celebrated as beautiful and good. The prayer for their removal is not vindictive but ecological — creation's perfection requires the removal of those who desecrate it. bārekî nafshî ʾet-YHWH hallĕlûyāh — bless the LORD, O my soul! Hallelujah! The return to the opening self-address (as in Ps 103:22) and the first hallĕlûyāh (praise-YHWH) in the Psalter — a compound formed from hallelû (praise ye!) + YAH (the abbreviated divine name). The creation hymn ends with the first hallelujah, launching the hallelujah psalms that increasingly dominate Psalms 105-150.
